words.definition: a move to a higher job with more responsibility
example_sentences: She received a promotion after just one year at the company.
He's been working towards a promotion for three years.
A promotion usually comes with a salary increase.
words.pronunciation_tip: pruh-MOH-shun — stress on second syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I got a higher job' — say 'I got a promotion'.

words.definition: the situation of losing your job because your position no longer exists
example_sentences: She was made redundant when the company downsized.
He received a redundancy package worth three months' salary.
Redundancy can be a chance to find a better job.
words.pronunciation_tip: rih-DUN-dun-see — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I was fired' — fired = dismissed for poor performance. Redundant = the job no longer exists.

words.definition: a formal assessment of someone's work performance
example_sentences: Her annual appraisal was very positive.
The manager conducted appraisals for every team member.
He was nervous before his performance appraisal.
words.pronunciation_tip: uh-PRAYZ-ul — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the review of my work' — say 'my appraisal' or 'performance review'.

words.definition: a document listing your education, experience, and skills (curriculum vitae)
example_sentences: She spent an evening updating her CV before applying.
His CV was impressive — five years of relevant experience.
Always tailor your CV to the specific job you're applying for.
words.pronunciation_tip: SEE-VEE — two letters, each said separately.
words.common_mistake: In American English this is called a 'résumé' (REZ-yoo-may). In British English: CV.

words.definition: building relationships with people who can help your career
example_sentences: Networking is essential in the creative industries.
She attended a conference mainly to do some networking.
Don't underestimate the power of networking.
words.pronunciation_tip: NET-wur-king — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'meeting important people' — say 'networking'.

words.definition: to formally leave a job by choice
example_sentences: She resigned from her position after fifteen years.
He resigned because he was offered a better opportunity.
I'm thinking of resigning if things don't improve.
words.pronunciation_tip: rih-ZYN — stress on second syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I quit my job' in formal contexts — say 'I resigned'.

words.definition: the amount of work someone has to do
example_sentences: Her workload has increased dramatically since the restructure.
He's struggling to manage his heavy workload.
The new software has reduced our workload considerably.
words.pronunciation_tip: WURK-lohd — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I have much work' — say 'I have a heavy workload'.

words.definition: giving tasks or responsibility to someone else
example_sentences: Effective delegation is a key management skill.
She delegates well — her team always knows what to do.
Poor delegation leads to managers being overworked.
words.pronunciation_tip: del-ih-GAY-shun — four syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'giving work to others' — say 'delegation'.

words.definition: a period at the start of a job during which you can be dismissed easily
example_sentences: He is still on probation for the first three months.
She passed her probation period with excellent feedback.
The contract specifies a six-month probation period.
words.pronunciation_tip: proh-BAY-shun — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'trial period' — say 'probation period' or 'on probation'.

words.definition: working independently for different clients rather than one employer
example_sentences: She works freelance as a graphic designer.
He went freelance after ten years in the same company.
Freelance work offers flexibility but no job security.
words.pronunciation_tip: FREE-lanss — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'he works alone' — say 'he works freelance' or 'he is a freelancer'.

words.definition: something that encourages people to work harder or perform better
example_sentences: The company offers bonuses as an incentive for high performance.
Financial incentives are not always the most effective.
What incentive is there to stay if the salary is so low?
words.pronunciation_tip: in-SEN-tiv — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'something that makes people want to work' — say 'incentive'.

words.definition: the rate at which employees leave a company and are replaced
example_sentences: High staff turnover is a sign of poor management.
The company has very low turnover — most people stay for years.
High turnover is costly and disrupts team performance.
words.pronunciation_tip: TUR-noh-ver — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse staff turnover (employees leaving) with financial turnover (total sales).

words.definition: the ability to decide and act on your own without being told
example_sentences: She shows great initiative — she never waits to be told.
Take the initiative and volunteer for new projects.
Employers value people who show initiative.
words.pronunciation_tip: ih-NISH-ee-uh-tiv — five syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'she works without being told' as a noun — say 'she shows initiative'.

words.definition: a standard against which something is measured or compared
example_sentences: The company uses industry benchmarks to measure performance.
Their customer service has become the benchmark for the sector.
We need to benchmark our results against competitors.
words.pronunciation_tip: BENCH-mark — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'standard for comparison' every time — say 'benchmark'.

words.definition: a list of the best candidates selected from all applicants
example_sentences: She was delighted to be on the shortlist for the award.
They shortlisted five candidates for interview.
Getting onto the shortlist is already an achievement.
words.pronunciation_tip: SHAWT-list — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I was chosen from the list' — say 'I was shortlisted'.

words.definition: to pay another company to do work rather than doing it internally
example_sentences: They outsource their IT support to a specialist company.
Many businesses outsource customer service overseas.
Outsourcing can reduce costs but affects quality control.
words.pronunciation_tip: OWT-sawss — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'give work to another company' — say 'outsource'.

words.definition: a system where employees can choose their own working hours
example_sentences: The company offers flexi-time so parents can manage childcare.
She works flexi-time — she starts at 7am and finishes at 3pm.
Flexi-time improves work-life balance significantly.
words.pronunciation_tip: FLEK-see-tym — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'flexible hours' every time — say 'flexi-time'.

words.definition: approached by another company and offered a job
example_sentences: She was headhunted by a rival firm last year.
Being headhunted is a sign that your skills are valued.
He was headhunted away from the company for a huge salary.
words.pronunciation_tip: HED-hun-tid — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'another company called me with a job offer' — say 'I was headhunted'.

words.definition: to work with others to achieve a shared goal
example_sentences: The two departments collaborated on the new project.
She collaborates really well with people from different backgrounds.
We need to collaborate more effectively across teams.
words.pronunciation_tip: kuh-LAB-uh-rayt — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'work together' always — say 'collaborate' in professional contexts.

words.definition: having more education or experience than a job requires
example_sentences: She felt overqualified for the entry-level position.
Employers sometimes reject candidates they think are overqualified.
Being overqualified can actually work against you in interviews.
words.pronunciation_tip: oh-ver-KWOL-ih-fyd — five syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'she has too much experience for the job' — say 'she is overqualified'.
